chunk_summary: This section provides a framework for addressing nutrition myths in India, categorized by risk tiers, with culturally sensitive communication strategies and safe dietary replacements.
learning_objectives: Understand the classification of nutrition myths into risk tiers and their implications.
Learn culturally sensitive communication techniques for myth correction using motivational interviewing (MI).
Identify safe dietary replacements for common nutrition myths in India.
Promote evidence-based practices for infant and adolescent nutrition.
key_facts: Honey should not be given to infants under 12 months due to botulism risk.
Complementary feeding should begin at 6 months to avoid growth compromise.
Stopping feeds during diarrhea can worsen dehydration; smaller, thicker feeds are recommended.
Tier 2 myths, such as reliance on 'height powders' or packaged juices, waste money and displace nutrition.
Motivational interviewing (elicit–provide–elicit) helps avoid caregiver humiliation and fosters collaboration.
Practical alternatives include replacing honey with mashed fruit for infants and juice with whole fruit or chaas without added sugar.
reference_quote: Infant and young child feeding practices should align with WHO guidelines, including avoiding honey under 12 months due to botulism risk. (Reference 1)
document_key_topics: Safe dietary replacements for common myths
Evidence-based infant and adolescent nutrition practices
